title: Empyema
oneLiner: Infected pleural space (pus or positive culture) complicating pneumonia, lung abscess, or thoracic surgery; requires drainage and antibiotics to prevent sepsis and fibrothorax.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are called to review a 72-year-old man on day 7 of treatment for community-acquired pneumonia. He had originally improved on IV amoxicillin, but for the last 48 hours his temperature spiked again to 38.7 °C, he has a drenching night sweats, and he complains of sharp right-sided chest pain that worsens when he takes a deep breath or coughs. On examination he appears toxic, his respiratory rate is 26, and he has reduced chest expansion on the right with stony dullness to percussion, diminished breath sounds, and egophony at the base. His oxygen saturations are 89% on room air. His white cell count is 19 × 10⁹/L with a left shift, and his CRP is 290 mg/L.

**Pattern clues dissected:**

- **Clinical failure to improve after initial response** → suggests a complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema developing despite antibiotics.
- **Recurrence of fever + night sweats** → systemic inflammatory response to pus in the pleural space; note that fevers may be low-grade but persistent.
- **Sharp pleuritic chest pain** → inflammation of the parietal pleura; also suggests the effusion is not yet massive enough to completely splint the lung.
- **Toxic appearance + tachypnoea** → sepsis physiology from the infected collection.
- **Stony dullness on percussion** → distinguishes an effusion from consolidation (which is dull, not stony). Dullness is absolute and persists despite changes in position.
- **Diminished breath sounds + egophony** → fluid dampens lung sounds but high-pitched "E-to-A" change indicates underlying consolidated lung (e.g., effusion overlying pneumonic consolidation).
- **High inflammatory markers** → ongoing infection; leukocytosis with left shift = bacterial, CRP >100 = significant inflammation.
- **Hypoxia** → V/Q mismatch from lung compression and underlying pneumonia.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Subacute empyema in the elderly or immunocompromised:** only weight loss, night sweats, and a non-productive cough – no fever or chest pain. Often misdiagnosed as TB or malignancy. Check pleural fluid in any unilateral pleural effusion of unknown cause.
- **Post-surgical empyema (e.g., after thoracic surgery or oesophagectomy):** presents with persistent low-grade fever, raised white cells, and a persistent air leak; classic “pus from the drain” but may be occult if drain is not in the right place.
- **Anaerobe-predominant empyema:** foul-smelling breath, putrid sputum, and a chronic, indolent course. Often associated with aspiration pneumonia (alcoholism, poor dentition, swallowing disorders). May not have high fevers.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Empyema is the presence of pus (macroscopic or microbiological) in the pleural space — essentially a pleural abscess. It is defined by pleural fluid that is frankly purulent, or culture-positive, or has strongly exudative biochemistry (pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, LDH >1000 U/L). The British Thoracic Society (BTS) classifies pleural infection into three stages: (1) simple parapneumonic effusion (sterile, pH >7.2), (2) complicated parapneumonic effusion (pH ≤7.2 but not yet frankly purulent), and (3) empyema (pus). Stage 3 is the classic “surgical” disease.

This matters clinically because empyema is a common (up to 20% of hospitalised pneumonias develop a parapneumonic effusion, and 10–15% of those progress to empyema) and dangerous condition. Mortality is 10–20% even with optimal management. Common pitfalls include not recognising failure to respond, not draining early enough (leading to loculation and fibrothorax), and using inadequate antibiotics for the anaerobic component. A delay in source control is the single biggest driver of poor outcomes.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
The story begins with a pulmonary infection – most often bacterial pneumonia – that extends to the visceral pleural surface. The pleural space normally contains only a thin film of fluid and has a strong ability to absorb small effusions. But when bacteria invade, the pleural mesothelial cells are activated, increasing vascular permeability and exudation of protein-rich fluid (simple parapneumonic effusion – stage 1). If host defences fail to clear the bacteria in this fluid, a fibrinopurulent reaction occurs (stage 2): neutrophils pour in, bacteria proliferate, and fibrin is deposited over the visceral and parietal pleura, creating loculations and septations. This makes the fluid thick and difficult to drain through a small tube. Eventually, the fluid becomes frankly purulent (stage 3 – empyema) with a pH <7.2, low glucose (consumed by bacteria and leukocytes), and high LDH (cell destruction).

**Why each key clinical sign happens:**

- **Pus =** bacteria and necrotic neutrophils. Macroscopically purulent fluid is the defining feature; even if culture-negative (e.g., after antibiotics), the presence of pus confirms empyema.
- **Stony dullness:** The pleural space is filled with dense, proteinaceous material that impedes sound transmission more than simple transudate. This is a very specific sign.
- **Absent breath sounds / bronchial breathing at the top of the effusion:** Fluid dampens sound, but above the fluid level you may hear bronchial breathing if consolidated lung is pressed against the chest wall (the “squelch” sign of a large effusion compressing underlying lung).
- **Egophony =** compressed, atelectatic lung behind the fluid conducts high-frequency sounds better; you hear “E” as “A”.
- **Pleuritic pain:** The parietal pleura is innervated by intercostal nerves; infection and inflammation stretch it during inspiration → sharp, localised pain.
- **Fever + systemic toxicity:** Bacterial products and cytokines enter the systemic circulation from the infected pleural space, causing a septic state.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The patient who initially improved and then deteriorated is moving through stages 1→2→3. The failure to resolve with antibiotics alone means the infection has become isolated from the bloodstream in a loculated, acidic pocket where antibiotics penetrate poorly.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue / Context |
|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Infectious (most common)** | Community-acquired pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, anaerobes) | “Pneumonia not improving after 48–72h of IV antibiotics” |
| | Hospital-acquired pneumonia / ventilator-associated | “ICU patient with fever and new effusion”; often Gram-negatives (Klebsiella, E. coli) or MRSA |
| | Anaerobic infection (aspiration pneumonia) | “Poor dentition, alcoholism, dysphagia, reduced consciousness” – putrid odour |
| | Tuberculosis | “Chronic cough, night sweats, weight loss, lymphocytic effusion” |
| | Fungal (rare: Histoplasma, Aspergillus) | Immunosuppressed host, HIV, haematological malignancy |
| **Iatrogenic / Post-procedural** | Post-thoracic surgery (lung resection, oesophagectomy) | “Persistent air leak, rising WCC, new fever post-op” |
| | Chest drain insertion (contaminated drain) | “Patient developed new effusion after chest drain inserted for pneumothorax” |
| | Oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave syndrome) | “Violent vomiting, chest pain, surgical emphysema, left-sided effusion” – highly virulent mixed flora |
| **Trauma** | Penetrating chest injury / haemothorax | “Stab or GSW to chest, retained haemothorax becomes infected” |
| **Abdominal infection** | Subphrenic abscess / liver abscess | “Right upper quadrant pain, swinging fevers, referred shoulder pain, right pleural effusion” – sympathetic effusion may become infected |
| **Miscellaneous** | Connective tissue disease (rare – rheumatoid pleurisy) | “Known RA, chronic exudative effusion that fails to resolve” – often sterile, but can become superinfected |
| | Malignancy (secondary infection of malignant effusion) | “Known lung cancer, recurrent effusion, positive pleural cytology” – empyema as a complication of chemo/immunosuppression |

**Most common cause:** The commonest is non-anaerobic community-acquired pneumonia in adults (Streptococcus pneumoniae is the single most common organism, but anaerobes are frequently co-pathogens). In children, S. aureus is more common.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M: **Diagnostic criteria (BTS guidelines):** Pleural fluid pH ≤7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, LDH >1000 U/L, or frankly purulent fluid on aspiration. The presence of pus is diagnostic regardless of biochemistry.
sections.Investigation approach:
| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Opacification +/- meniscus sign; large effusion, may show air-fluid level if bronchopleural fistula | First-line to confirm pleural fluid; easy, cheap | An opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift suggests massive effusion. Dullness is not specific – ultrasound is better. |
| **Ultrasound chest** | Anechoic, complex septated, or echogenic fluid. Can show loculations, thick parietal pleura, “snow globe” effusion | Better than CXR to differentiate effusion from consolidation; guides thoracentesis; detects loculation | Loculated = more likely complicated. Ultrasound reduces pneumothorax risk during aspiration. |
| **CT thorax with contrast** | Pleural enhancement, split pleura sign (enhanced visceral and parietal pleura separated by fluid), loculation, underlying lung abscess or mass | Confirms empyema vs. other effusion; defines extent; pre-surgical planning | Split pleura sign is highly specific for empyema. Also images consolidation, abscess, oesophageal cause. |
| **Diagnostic thoracentesis** | Obtain fluid: pus (macroscopic), cloudy/seropurulent; measure pH, glucose, LDH, protein, Gram stain, culture (aerobic + anaerobic), cell count, differential | Gold standard to diagnose empyema. Must be done in all suspected cases unless contraindicated | **Confirming finding:** Frank pus. **Ruling out:** If pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, negative Gram stain, and clear fluid → simple effusion (still need clinical correlation). |
| **Pleural fluid microbiology** | Gram stain (organism morphology), culture + sensitivity, PCR (for TB) | Identifies pathogen to guide antibiotics | Positive culture in 60% (lower if prior antibiotics); anaerobes need specific transport. |
| **Blood cultures** | Bacteraemia | Identifies systemic spread; 5–10% positive | Positive blood culture may help if pleural fluid sterile. |
| **Blood inflammatory markers** | WCC, CRP, procalcitonin | Monitor response; decide need for escalation | Very high CRP >200 suggests ongoing infection. Fall <50% in 48h after drainage = poor source control. |
| **Pleural fluid LDH** | Reflects cell turnover | Distinguishes exudate from transudate (Light’s criteria) + helps stage | LDH >1000 = certainly complicated effusion. |

**Single finding that confirms empyema:** Aspiration of macroscopically purulent pleural fluid. **Single finding that rules it out:** Clear, straw-coloured fluid with pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, LDH <500, and negative Gram stain – though still watch if clinical suspicion is high.
sections.Immediate (first hour):
1. **Assess for sepsis:** Check NEWS2, lactate, blood cultures. If signs of septic shock (lactate >2, hypotension after fluids, vasopressor requirement) – start IV fluids, call ICU. Do not delay drainage for stabilisation if feasible.
2. **Empiric IV antibiotics** – must cover the likely pathogens:
   - Community-acquired & aspiration: **Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV every 8 hours** (broad cover including anaerobes and Strep). *Alternative if penicillin allergic: Cefuroxime 1.5g IV every 8 hours + Metronidazole 500mg IV every 8 hours.*
   - Hospital-acquired / post-surgical: **Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV every 6 hours** (covers Pseudomonas, anaerobic). *Alternative: Ceftazidime 2g IV every 8 hours + Metronidazole.*
   - MRSA risk (known colonisation, prolonged hospital stay): Add **Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 hours** (trough 10–15 mg/L) or Linezolid.
3. **Urgent ultrasound-guided thoracentesis** – even if only to confirm pus and take samples. If the effusion is large and causing respiratory compromise, proceed directly to drain insertion.

**Why?** Every hour without drainage increases loculation risk. Antibiotics alone cannot clear a loculated pocket of pus.
sections.Ward (24–72h):
1. **Chest drain insertion** – image-guided (ultrasound or CT) is preferred. Use a wide-bore tube (20–28F) for thick pus – small-bore drains frequently block. If the fluid is thin and non-loculated, a 12–14F drain may be adequate, but monitor closely for occlusion.
2. **Intrapleural fibrinolytics** if loculations are present (ultrasound shows septations). BTS guidelines: **Tissue plasminogen activator (alteplase) 10 mg + DNase 5 mg** in 100 mL normal saline via drain, clamp for 1 hour, then flush. Repeat daily for 1–3 days. This combination dissolves fibrin plugs and reduces viscosity; it improved drainage and reduced need for surgery in the MIST-2 trial.
3. **Monitor drain output** – >100 mL/day and clinical improvement (temp down, inflammatory markers falling) suggest adequate drainage. If output <50 mL/day but ultrasound still shows collection → drain blockage or loculation → consider CT or VATS.
4. **Adjust antibiotics based on culture results.** If no organism grows (common after prior antibiotics), continue broad-spectrum cover for 3–4 weeks. Anaerobic coverage is mandatory.
5. **Pain control** – pleural drains are painful. Use regular paracetamol + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg TDS) if no contraindications. Avoid over-sedation that impairs cough and sputum clearance.
6. **Respiratory physiotherapy** – incentive spirometry, deep breathing exercises, coughing techniques to re-expand the lung and obliterate pleural space.
sections.Long-term (beyond 1 week):
1. **If chest drain fails** (loculated, persistent sepsis, output >100 mL/day after 7 days) – refer for **Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS)** or **open decortication**. VATS is first-line for stage 2/3 empyema if medical management fails within 48–72h.
2. **Duration of antibiotics** – minimum 2 weeks IV, then switch to oral (guided by sensitivities) for a total of 4–6 weeks. Common oral step-down: **Co-amoxiclav 625mg PO TDS** or **Clindamycin 450mg PO QDS** if aspiration. Ensure good coverage.
3. **Follow-up imaging** – CXR at 4–6 weeks to ensure resolution. CT if persistent pleural thickening or suspicion of fibrothorax.
4. **Identify underlying cause** – bronchoscopy if suspected lung abscess, foreign body, or malignancy. Consider TB investigations if chronic or if from TB-endemic area.
sections.What NOT to do (common pitfalls):
- **Do not use a small-bore (8–10F) drain for thick pus** – it will block. Start with 20–28F and if the fluid thins, you can downsize later.
- **Do not remove the drain before the lung is re-expanded and output <100 mL/day** – you risk re-accumulation.
- **Do not forget anaerobic coverage** – anaerobes are present in >50% of empyemas and are often missed.
- **Do not rely on antibiotics alone** – empyema is a surgical disease; drainage is the definitive treatment.
- **Do not wait too long for medical management to work** – if no improvement in 48–72h despite drain and fibrinolytics, escalate to surgery.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Empyema | Parapneumonic effusion (simple) | Lung abscess | Pleural effusion (malignant) |
|---------|---------|------------------------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp pleuritic, persistent | Dull ache, may be absent | Dull / deep, may radiate to shoulder | Usually none, may be dull if large |
| **Exam** | Toxic, febrile, stony dullness, egophony | Febrile, less toxic, dullness | Cough with foul sputum, no typical pleural signs | Afebrile, weight loss, non- tender dullness |
| **CXR** | Uniform opacification with meniscus, possibly loculated | Smaller opacification, meniscus | Cavity with air-fluid level (lung parenchyma) | Large effusion, often unilateral, mediastinal shift |
| **Pleural fluid** | Pus, pH <7.2, glucose <2.2, LDH >1000 | Clear, pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, LDH <500 | Lack of fluid (if lung abscess only, no effusion) | Straw-coloured or bloody, exudate by Light’s, cytology positive |
| **Gram stain / culture** | Positive or purulent | Negative | Blood or sputum culture may be positive when abscess ruptures | Negative for bacteria |
| **Key differentiator** | **Pus on aspiration** – that’s it | Biochemically benign, pH >7.2 | **Cavity on CT within lung**, not in pleural space | **Cytology or pleural biopsy shows malignant cells** |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
1. **Empyema necessitans** – pus tracks through the chest wall to form a subcutaneous abscess or draining sinus. Presents as a superficial chest wall swelling that may feel warm and fluctuant. Classic sign: “pointing” swelling on the anterior chest. Management: incision and drainage + definitive surgery for the empyema (VATS/decortication).
2. **Bronchopleural fistula** – communication between bronchus and pleural space. Presents with sudden large air leak from the chest drain, persistent pneumothorax, or tension physiology. CXR shows new air-fluid level. **What to do:** Call senior immediately – needs surgical repair (VATS or open) and prolonged drainage.
3. **Sepsis / septic shock** – the patient becomes hypotensive, febrile, tachycardic, with rising lactate. This is a medical emergency: start resuscitative fluids, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (if not already), call ICU, and ensure the chest drain is patent to decompress the source. If the drain is occluded, emergency percutaneous or surgical drainage is needed.
4. **Respiratory failure** – large, loculated empyema compresses the lung and causes restrictive ventilatory defect. Leads to hypoxia, hypercapnia, and need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation. Late presentation: patient is dyspnoeic at rest, tripoding, cyanosed. **Immediate action:** drain the effusion and consider respiratory support.
5. **Fibrothorax** – if a chronic empyema is not treated, the fibrin layers organise into a thick, inelastic peel that encases the lung and prevents re-expansion. Presents months to years later with persistent chest tightness, restrictive spirometry, and reduced hemithorax volume on CXR. **Management:** decortication is the only option; it is major surgery with high morbidity.
6. **Chronic empyema** – indolent, untreated empyema becomes a chronic infected cavity. Patient may have persistent low-grade fever, weight loss, clubbing, and an unremitting productive cough. Diagnosis by CT showing a thick-walled loculated effusion with split pleura. Needs open drainage (Eloesser flap) or decortication.

**Late-presentation patterns – exam stems that test “what happens if untreated”:**
- A patient from a low-resource setting presents with years of cough, weight loss, chest pain, and now has a draining sinus on his chest wall with purulent discharge – empyema necessitans from chronic TB empyema.
- A post-pneumonia patient returns 6 months later with recurrent chest infections and a small-volume, thickened hemithorax – fibrothorax.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **DRAIN** – for management steps: **D**rain the pus (chest tube or surgery), **R**x antibiotics (IV + oral step-down), **A**nalgesia, **I**nvestigate underlying cause, **N**utrition support.
- **P-U-S** – for fluid characteristics of empyema: **P**urulent, **U**nder pH 7.2, **S**ugars low (glucose <2.2).
- **EMPIE** – for causes: **E**mpyema (obvious), **M**alignancy, **P**neumonia/anaerobes, **I**atrogenic (post-surgery/drain), **E**sophageal perforation.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“The patient who fails to improve on antibiotics for pneumonia”** – always get a chest ultrasound or CT to look for an effusion that needs draining. Do not just change antibiotics without imaging.
2. **Cardinal sign of organisational failure** – if the chest drain stops draining but the patient remains febrile, the drain is blocked or there is another loculus. Never assume “it has all drained out” without an ultrasound check.
3. **Anaerobes are silent** – if the fluid is odourless on Gram stain, you may still have anaerobes. Always send a separate anaerobic culture bottle, and cover empirically.
4. **Small-bore drains are not always better** – for empyema, a large-bore (20–28F) drain is less likely to block and more effective for thick pus. Do not be afraid of the pain; manage with local anaesthetic and analgesia.
5. **Ultrasound is the extension of your stethoscope** – a bedside lung ultrasound can differentiate simple from complex effusions in seconds. Learn point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) if you can.
6. **The split pleura sign on CT** (enhancing visceral and parietal pleura separated by fluid) is pathognomonic for empyema – if you see this, plan for surgery if medical management fails.
7. **Time is lung** – each day of undrained empyema increases the risk of decortication. If the drain + fibrinolytics are not working in 48h, escalate to VATS.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering a CXR to “follow the effusion” without having a diagnostic tap – you may miss the chance to drain early.
- Removing the chest drain early because output is low, without confirming lung re-expansion on CXR.
- Delaying surgery in a frail elderly patient – age alone is not a contraindication to VATS. Outcomes are worse with prolonged medical management.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | Action | Call who? |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if single high parameter) or lactate >2 mmol/L | Start sepsis 6, fluid bolus, blood cultures, IV antibiotics | Immediate review by medical registrar ± anaesthesia/ICU registrar |
| Septic shock (hypotension despite fluids, need for vasopressors) | Call ICU team, consider central line, start noradrenaline; organise urgent bedside drainage | Crash team / ICU consultant |
| Respiratory failure (PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 0.5, or PaCO2 >6.5, or RR >30, or

rawMarkdown:
# Empyema

**One-liner:** Infected pleural space (pus or positive culture) complicating pneumonia, lung abscess, or thoracic surgery; requires drainage and antibiotics to prevent sepsis and fibrothorax.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

You are called to review a 72-year-old man on day 7 of treatment for community-acquired pneumonia. He had originally improved on IV amoxicillin, but for the last 48 hours his temperature spiked again to 38.7 °C, he has a drenching night sweats, and he complains of sharp right-sided chest pain that worsens when he takes a deep breath or coughs. On examination he appears toxic, his respiratory rate is 26, and he has reduced chest expansion on the right with stony dullness to percussion, diminished breath sounds, and egophony at the base. His oxygen saturations are 89% on room air. His white cell count is 19 × 10⁹/L with a left shift, and his CRP is 290 mg/L.

**Pattern clues dissected:**

- **Clinical failure to improve after initial response** → suggests a complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema developing despite antibiotics.
- **Recurrence of fever + night sweats** → systemic inflammatory response to pus in the pleural space; note that fevers may be low-grade but persistent.
- **Sharp pleuritic chest pain** → inflammation of the parietal pleura; also suggests the effusion is not yet massive enough to completely splint the lung.
- **Toxic appearance + tachypnoea** → sepsis physiology from the infected collection.
- **Stony dullness on percussion** → distinguishes an effusion from consolidation (which is dull, not stony). Dullness is absolute and persists despite changes in position.
- **Diminished breath sounds + egophony** → fluid dampens lung sounds but high-pitched "E-to-A" change indicates underlying consolidated lung (e.g., effusion overlying pneumonic consolidation).
- **High inflammatory markers** → ongoing infection; leukocytosis with left shift = bacterial, CRP >100 = significant inflammation.
- **Hypoxia** → V/Q mismatch from lung compression and underlying pneumonia.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Subacute empyema in the elderly or immunocompromised:** only weight loss, night sweats, and a non-productive cough – no fever or chest pain. Often misdiagnosed as TB or malignancy. Check pleural fluid in any unilateral pleural effusion of unknown cause.
- **Post-surgical empyema (e.g., after thoracic surgery or oesophagectomy):** presents with persistent low-grade fever, raised white cells, and a persistent air leak; classic “pus from the drain” but may be occult if drain is not in the right place.
- **Anaerobe-predominant empyema:** foul-smelling breath, putrid sputum, and a chronic, indolent course. Often associated with aspiration pneumonia (alcoholism, poor dentition, swallowing disorders). May not have high fevers.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Empyema is the presence of pus (macroscopic or microbiological) in the pleural space — essentially a pleural abscess. It is defined by pleural fluid that is frankly purulent, or culture-positive, or has strongly exudative biochemistry (pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, LDH >1000 U/L). The British Thoracic Society (BTS) classifies pleural infection into three stages: (1) simple parapneumonic effusion (sterile, pH >7.2), (2) complicated parapneumonic effusion (pH ≤7.2 but not yet frankly purulent), and (3) empyema (pus). Stage 3 is the classic “surgical” disease.

This matters clinically because empyema is a common (up to 20% of hospitalised pneumonias develop a parapneumonic effusion, and 10–15% of those progress to empyema) and dangerous condition. Mortality is 10–20% even with optimal management. Common pitfalls include not recognising failure to respond, not draining early enough (leading to loculation and fibrothorax), and using inadequate antibiotics for the anaerobic component. A delay in source control is the single biggest driver of poor outcomes.

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

The story begins with a pulmonary infection – most often bacterial pneumonia – that extends to the visceral pleural surface. The pleural space normally contains only a thin film of fluid and has a strong ability to absorb small effusions. But when bacteria invade, the pleural mesothelial cells are activated, increasing vascular permeability and exudation of protein-rich fluid (simple parapneumonic effusion – stage 1). If host defences fail to clear the bacteria in this fluid, a fibrinopurulent reaction occurs (stage 2): neutrophils pour in, bacteria proliferate, and fibrin is deposited over the visceral and parietal pleura, creating loculations and septations. This makes the fluid thick and difficult to drain through a small tube. Eventually, the fluid becomes frankly purulent (stage 3 – empyema) with a pH <7.2, low glucose (consumed by bacteria and leukocytes), and high LDH (cell destruction).

**Why each key clinical sign happens:**

- **Pus =** bacteria and necrotic neutrophils. Macroscopically purulent fluid is the defining feature; even if culture-negative (e.g., after antibiotics), the presence of pus confirms empyema.
- **Stony dullness:** The pleural space is filled with dense, proteinaceous material that impedes sound transmission more than simple transudate. This is a very specific sign.
- **Absent breath sounds / bronchial breathing at the top of the effusion:** Fluid dampens sound, but above the fluid level you may hear bronchial breathing if consolidated lung is pressed against the chest wall (the “squelch” sign of a large effusion compressing underlying lung).
- **Egophony =** compressed, atelectatic lung behind the fluid conducts high-frequency sounds better; you hear “E” as “A”.
- **Pleuritic pain:** The parietal pleura is innervated by intercostal nerves; infection and inflammation stretch it during inspiration → sharp, localised pain.
- **Fever + systemic toxicity:** Bacterial products and cytokines enter the systemic circulation from the infected pleural space, causing a septic state.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The patient who initially improved and then deteriorated is moving through stages 1→2→3. The failure to resolve with antibiotics alone means the infection has become isolated from the bloodstream in a loculated, acidic pocket where antibiotics penetrate poorly.

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue / Context |
|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Infectious (most common)** | Community-acquired pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, anaerobes) | “Pneumonia not improving after 48–72h of IV antibiotics” |
| | Hospital-acquired pneumonia / ventilator-associated | “ICU patient with fever and new effusion”; often Gram-negatives (Klebsiella, E. coli) or MRSA |
| | Anaerobic infection (aspiration pneumonia) | “Poor dentition, alcoholism, dysphagia, reduced consciousness” – putrid odour |
| | Tuberculosis | “Chronic cough, night sweats, weight loss, lymphocytic effusion” |
| | Fungal (rare: Histoplasma, Aspergillus) | Immunosuppressed host, HIV, haematological malignancy |
| **Iatrogenic / Post-procedural** | Post-thoracic surgery (lung resection, oesophagectomy) | “Persistent air leak, rising WCC, new fever post-op” |
| | Chest drain insertion (contaminated drain) | “Patient developed new effusion after chest drain inserted for pneumothorax” |
| | Oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave syndrome) | “Violent vomiting, chest pain, surgical emphysema, left-sided effusion” – highly virulent mixed flora |
| **Trauma** | Penetrating chest injury / haemothorax | “Stab or GSW to chest, retained haemothorax becomes infected” |
| **Abdominal infection** | Subphrenic abscess / liver abscess | “Right upper quadrant pain, swinging fevers, referred shoulder pain, right pleural effusion” – sympathetic effusion may become infected |
| **Miscellaneous** | Connective tissue disease (rare – rheumatoid pleurisy) | “Known RA, chronic exudative effusion that fails to resolve” – often sterile, but can become superinfected |
| | Malignancy (secondary infection of malignant effusion) | “Known lung cancer, recurrent effusion, positive pleural cytology” – empyema as a complication of chemo/immunosuppression |

**Most common cause:** The commonest is non-anaerobic community-acquired pneumonia in adults (Streptococcus pneumoniae is the single most common organism, but anaerobes are frequently co-pathogens). In children, S. aureus is more common.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (BTS guidelines):** Pleural fluid pH ≤7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, LDH >1000 U/L, or frankly purulent fluid on aspiration. The presence of pus is diagnostic regardless of biochemistry.

### Investigation approach

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Opacification +/- meniscus sign; large effusion, may show air-fluid level if bronchopleural fistula | First-line to confirm pleural fluid; easy, cheap | An opaque hemithorax with mediastinal shift suggests massive effusion. Dullness is not specific – ultrasound is better. |
| **Ultrasound chest** | Anechoic, complex septated, or echogenic fluid. Can show loculations, thick parietal pleura, “snow globe” effusion | Better than CXR to differentiate effusion from consolidation; guides thoracentesis; detects loculation | Loculated = more likely complicated. Ultrasound reduces pneumothorax risk during aspiration. |
| **CT thorax with contrast** | Pleural enhancement, split pleura sign (enhanced visceral and parietal pleura separated by fluid), loculation, underlying lung abscess or mass | Confirms empyema vs. other effusion; defines extent; pre-surgical planning | Split pleura sign is highly specific for empyema. Also images consolidation, abscess, oesophageal cause. |
| **Diagnostic thoracentesis** | Obtain fluid: pus (macroscopic), cloudy/seropurulent; measure pH, glucose, LDH, protein, Gram stain, culture (aerobic + anaerobic), cell count, differential | Gold standard to diagnose empyema. Must be done in all suspected cases unless contraindicated | **Confirming finding:** Frank pus. **Ruling out:** If pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, negative Gram stain, and clear fluid → simple effusion (still need clinical correlation). |
| **Pleural fluid microbiology** | Gram stain (organism morphology), culture + sensitivity, PCR (for TB) | Identifies pathogen to guide antibiotics | Positive culture in 60% (lower if prior antibiotics); anaerobes need specific transport. |
| **Blood cultures** | Bacteraemia | Identifies systemic spread; 5–10% positive | Positive blood culture may help if pleural fluid sterile. |
| **Blood inflammatory markers** | WCC, CRP, procalcitonin | Monitor response; decide need for escalation | Very high CRP >200 suggests ongoing infection. Fall <50% in 48h after drainage = poor source control. |
| **Pleural fluid LDH** | Reflects cell turnover | Distinguishes exudate from transudate (Light’s criteria) + helps stage | LDH >1000 = certainly complicated effusion. |

**Single finding that confirms empyema:** Aspiration of macroscopically purulent pleural fluid. **Single finding that rules it out:** Clear, straw-coloured fluid with pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, LDH <500, and negative Gram stain – though still watch if clinical suspicion is high.

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### Immediate (first hour)

1. **Assess for sepsis:** Check NEWS2, lactate, blood cultures. If signs of septic shock (lactate >2, hypotension after fluids, vasopressor requirement) – start IV fluids, call ICU. Do not delay drainage for stabilisation if feasible.
2. **Empiric IV antibiotics** – must cover the likely pathogens:
   - Community-acquired & aspiration: **Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV every 8 hours** (broad cover including anaerobes and Strep). *Alternative if penicillin allergic: Cefuroxime 1.5g IV every 8 hours + Metronidazole 500mg IV every 8 hours.*
   - Hospital-acquired / post-surgical: **Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV every 6 hours** (covers Pseudomonas, anaerobic). *Alternative: Ceftazidime 2g IV every 8 hours + Metronidazole.*
   - MRSA risk (known colonisation, prolonged hospital stay): Add **Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 hours** (trough 10–15 mg/L) or Linezolid.
3. **Urgent ultrasound-guided thoracentesis** – even if only to confirm pus and take samples. If the effusion is large and causing respiratory compromise, proceed directly to drain insertion.

**Why?** Every hour without drainage increases loculation risk. Antibiotics alone cannot clear a loculated pocket of pus.

### Ward (24–72h)

1. **Chest drain insertion** – image-guided (ultrasound or CT) is preferred. Use a wide-bore tube (20–28F) for thick pus – small-bore drains frequently block. If the fluid is thin and non-loculated, a 12–14F drain may be adequate, but monitor closely for occlusion.
2. **Intrapleural fibrinolytics** if loculations are present (ultrasound shows septations). BTS guidelines: **Tissue plasminogen activator (alteplase) 10 mg + DNase 5 mg** in 100 mL normal saline via drain, clamp for 1 hour, then flush. Repeat daily for 1–3 days. This combination dissolves fibrin plugs and reduces viscosity; it improved drainage and reduced need for surgery in the MIST-2 trial.
3. **Monitor drain output** – >100 mL/day and clinical improvement (temp down, inflammatory markers falling) suggest adequate drainage. If output <50 mL/day but ultrasound still shows collection → drain blockage or loculation → consider CT or VATS.
4. **Adjust antibiotics based on culture results.** If no organism grows (common after prior antibiotics), continue broad-spectrum cover for 3–4 weeks. Anaerobic coverage is mandatory.
5. **Pain control** – pleural drains are painful. Use regular paracetamol + NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg TDS) if no contraindications. Avoid over-sedation that impairs cough and sputum clearance.
6. **Respiratory physiotherapy** – incentive spirometry, deep breathing exercises, coughing techniques to re-expand the lung and obliterate pleural space.

### Long-term (beyond 1 week)

1. **If chest drain fails** (loculated, persistent sepsis, output >100 mL/day after 7 days) – refer for **Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS)** or **open decortication**. VATS is first-line for stage 2/3 empyema if medical management fails within 48–72h.
2. **Duration of antibiotics** – minimum 2 weeks IV, then switch to oral (guided by sensitivities) for a total of 4–6 weeks. Common oral step-down: **Co-amoxiclav 625mg PO TDS** or **Clindamycin 450mg PO QDS** if aspiration. Ensure good coverage.
3. **Follow-up imaging** – CXR at 4–6 weeks to ensure resolution. CT if persistent pleural thickening or suspicion of fibrothorax.
4. **Identify underlying cause** – bronchoscopy if suspected lung abscess, foreign body, or malignancy. Consider TB investigations if chronic or if from TB-endemic area.

### What NOT to do (common pitfalls)

- **Do not use a small-bore (8–10F) drain for thick pus** – it will block. Start with 20–28F and if the fluid thins, you can downsize later.
- **Do not remove the drain before the lung is re-expanded and output <100 mL/day** – you risk re-accumulation.
- **Do not forget anaerobic coverage** – anaerobes are present in >50% of empyemas and are often missed.
- **Do not rely on antibiotics alone** – empyema is a surgical disease; drainage is the definitive treatment.
- **Do not wait too long for medical management to work** – if no improvement in 48–72h despite drain and fibrinolytics, escalate to surgery.

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Empyema | Parapneumonic effusion (simple) | Lung abscess | Pleural effusion (malignant) |
|---------|---------|------------------------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp pleuritic, persistent | Dull ache, may be absent | Dull / deep, may radiate to shoulder | Usually none, may be dull if large |
| **Exam** | Toxic, febrile, stony dullness, egophony | Febrile, less toxic, dullness | Cough with foul sputum, no typical pleural signs | Afebrile, weight loss, non- tender dullness |
| **CXR** | Uniform opacification with meniscus, possibly loculated | Smaller opacification, meniscus | Cavity with air-fluid level (lung parenchyma) | Large effusion, often unilateral, mediastinal shift |
| **Pleural fluid** | Pus, pH <7.2, glucose <2.2, LDH >1000 | Clear, pH >7.2, glucose >2.2, LDH <500 | Lack of fluid (if lung abscess only, no effusion) | Straw-coloured or bloody, exudate by Light’s, cytology positive |
| **Gram stain / culture** | Positive or purulent | Negative | Blood or sputum culture may be positive when abscess ruptures | Negative for bacteria |
| **Key differentiator** | **Pus on aspiration** – that’s it | Biochemically benign, pH >7.2 | **Cavity on CT within lung**, not in pleural space | **Cytology or pleural biopsy shows malignant cells** |

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

1. **Empyema necessitans** – pus tracks through the chest wall to form a subcutaneous abscess or draining sinus. Presents as a superficial chest wall swelling that may feel warm and fluctuant. Classic sign: “pointing” swelling on the anterior chest. Management: incision and drainage + definitive surgery for the empyema (VATS/decortication).
2. **Bronchopleural fistula** – communication between bronchus and pleural space. Presents with sudden large air leak from the chest drain, persistent pneumothorax, or tension physiology. CXR shows new air-fluid level. **What to do:** Call senior immediately – needs surgical repair (VATS or open) and prolonged drainage.
3. **Sepsis / septic shock** – the patient becomes hypotensive, febrile, tachycardic, with rising lactate. This is a medical emergency: start resuscitative fluids, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (if not already), call ICU, and ensure the chest drain is patent to decompress the source. If the drain is occluded, emergency percutaneous or surgical drainage is needed.
4. **Respiratory failure** – large, loculated empyema compresses the lung and causes restrictive ventilatory defect. Leads to hypoxia, hypercapnia, and need for non-invasive ventilation or intubation. Late presentation: patient is dyspnoeic at rest, tripoding, cyanosed. **Immediate action:** drain the effusion and consider respiratory support.
5. **Fibrothorax** – if a chronic empyema is not treated, the fibrin layers organise into a thick, inelastic peel that encases the lung and prevents re-expansion. Presents months to years later with persistent chest tightness, restrictive spirometry, and reduced hemithorax volume on CXR. **Management:** decortication is the only option; it is major surgery with high morbidity.
6. **Chronic empyema** – indolent, untreated empyema becomes a chronic infected cavity. Patient may have persistent low-grade fever, weight loss, clubbing, and an unremitting productive cough. Diagnosis by CT showing a thick-walled loculated effusion with split pleura. Needs open drainage (Eloesser flap) or decortication.

**Late-presentation patterns – exam stems that test “what happens if untreated”:**
- A patient from a low-resource setting presents with years of cough, weight loss, chest pain, and now has a draining sinus on his chest wall with purulent discharge – empyema necessitans from chronic TB empyema.
- A post-pneumonia patient returns 6 months later with recurrent chest infections and a small-volume, thickened hemithorax – fibrothorax.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **DRAIN** – for management steps: **D**rain the pus (chest tube or surgery), **R**x antibiotics (IV + oral step-down), **A**nalgesia, **I**nvestigate underlying cause, **N**utrition support.
- **P-U-S** – for fluid characteristics of empyema: **P**urulent, **U**nder pH 7.2, **S**ugars low (glucose <2.2).
- **EMPIE** – for causes: **E**mpyema (obvious), **M**alignancy, **P**neumonia/anaerobes, **I**atrogenic (post-surgery/drain), **E**sophageal perforation.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“The patient who fails to improve on antibiotics for pneumonia”** – always get a chest ultrasound or CT to look for an effusion that needs draining. Do not just change antibiotics without imaging.
2. **Cardinal sign of organisational failure** – if the chest drain stops draining but the patient remains febrile, the drain is blocked or there is another loculus. Never assume “it has all drained out” without an ultrasound check.
3. **Anaerobes are silent** – if the fluid is odourless on Gram stain, you may still have anaerobes. Always send a separate anaerobic culture bottle, and cover empirically.
4. **Small-bore drains are not always better** – for empyema, a large-bore (20–28F) drain is less likely to block and more effective for thick pus. Do not be afraid of the pain; manage with local anaesthetic and analgesia.
5. **Ultrasound is the extension of your stethoscope** – a bedside lung ultrasound can differentiate simple from complex effusions in seconds. Learn point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) if you can.
6. **The split pleura sign on CT** (enhancing visceral and parietal pleura separated by fluid) is pathognomonic for empyema – if you see this, plan for surgery if medical management fails.
7. **Time is lung** – each day of undrained empyema increases the risk of decortication. If the drain + fibrinolytics are not working in 48h, escalate to VATS.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering a CXR to “follow the effusion” without having a diagnostic tap – you may miss the chance to drain early.
- Removing the chest drain early because output is low, without confirming lung re-expansion on CXR.
- Delaying surgery in a frail elderly patient – age alone is not a contraindication to VATS. Outcomes are worse with prolonged medical management.

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | Action | Call who? |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if single high parameter) or lactate >2 mmol/L | Start sepsis 6, fluid bolus, blood cultures, IV antibiotics | Immediate review by medical registrar ± anaesthesia/ICU registrar |
| Septic shock (hypotension despite fluids, need for vasopressors) | Call ICU team, consider central line, start noradrenaline; organise urgent bedside drainage | Crash team / ICU consultant |
| Respiratory failure (PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 0.5, or PaCO2 >6.5, or RR >30, or
